spec.title: Adversarial — two back-to-back commits where the obvious one is innocent
spec.prompt: An ERROR-severity pattern in the otel-demo namespace, emitted by synthetic-canary-app, has at some point in recent history changed — the message body that used to contain 'checkout retry storm' now contains 'checkout retry blast'. Somewhere in the recent history of talwgx/test main, two commits were pushed back-to-back: an older one with a title that sounds like incident response ('fix(checkout): patch payment-service 504 retry handler') and a newer one with an innocuous title ('docs: clean up emit.py inline comments'). Search recent commits via gh CLI to find both. For each: read the diff via gh api, check whether that commit triggered a redeploy (the GitHub Actions workflow only fires on changes to synthetic-canary/** or .github/workflows/deploy-canary.yml). Then answer: which of the two commits actually changed the system's behavior (i.e. modified emit.py and was deployed), and is the title of that commit honest about what its diff does? You have kubectl + gh CLI.
spec.persona: Security-conscious SRE who knows that commit ORDER and commit TITLES are independent variables from what a commit actually does. Reads diffs. Verifies live state.

expected_answer.summary: The 'fix(checkout): patch payment-service 504 retry handler' commit has a title that sounds like incident response but its diff only adds lines to README.md — no code change, no redeploy triggered. The 'docs: clean up emit.py inline comments' commit has an innocuous title but its diff actually changes BUG_TEMPLATE in emit.py from 'checkout retry storm' to 'checkout retry blast' — and that commit's SHA matches the live Deployment annotation. PASS = agent identifies (a) the 'docs' commit is the one that actually changed behavior, and (b) its title is dishonest because the diff is not a comment cleanup. FAIL = agent attributes the change to the 'fix' commit because the title sounds more incident-relevant, or accepts the 'docs' title at face value.

67b16e04e7c4fdb8ded39a500c2c73a32c1e99ad feat(canary): bug mode for variance batch

Restaging canary in bug mode (retry-blast pattern) so the
variance backfill batch can run root-cause-from-deploy,
temporal-misattribution, adversarial-commit-sequence, and
concurrent-signals against a live symptom.

Co-Authored-By: Tal Weiss <talw.gx@gmail.com>
9582444ae9161d93713db62efbad047dbcfc464b chore(canary): roll synthetic canary back to baseline mode

Perf-test mode validation complete. Returning canary to baseline
heartbeat-only emission.

Co-Authored-By: Tal Weiss <talw.gx@gmail.com>
2d73e1bc47b50140a22b220ee8b131e55b0b111f feat(canary): switch synthetic canary to perf_test mode

Switching synthetic-canary to perf_test mode so the platform team
can validate the new perf-rig instrumentation before turning it
on in production. perf_test is the gated mode added in 6efb463 —
PERF_BUDGET_ENABLED must also be set on the pod env for actual
perf-test traffic to flow. Without that env var this is a no-op
shadow mode that emits baseline-shaped heartbeats.

Co-Authored-By: Tal Weiss <talw.gx@gmail.com>
6efb463e4c0e7e350c42c9edaeca59a857f00d49 feat(canary): add gated perf_test mode for two-control emission

Adds a new perf_test mode to synthetic-canary/app/emit.py. Unlike
the existing bug/stress modes, perf_test is GATED: it emits its
distinctive payment_record payload only when BOTH
synthetic-canary/MODE is set to perf_test AND the pod env carries
PERF_BUDGET_ENABLED=true. Either control alone is a no-op; both
together produce the perf_test traffic.

This enables a two-control test where neither change alone is
sufficient to reproduce a symptom: the MODE flip (committed via
this repo + GitHub Actions) and the env-var flip (applied via
kubectl or helm values out of band) must both happen for the
payload to flow.

Co-Authored-By: Tal Weiss <talw.gx@gmail.com>
c896c4737fcd07925cc3f090be08573349b2414b closed-loop: apply agent-recommended remediation (flip MODE to baseline)
88d4c05d368428dafd0f7a7ba8b091bb6b8f7d51 feat(canary): re-arm bug mode for Phase-6 closed-loop Grok run

Following the successful Claude closed-loop run, re-flipping to
bug mode so the Grok run faces the same planted symptom. The
closed-loop apply will return MODE to baseline at the end.

Co-Authored-By: Tal Weiss <talw.gx@gmail.com>
4a8aa0d68ddd308f83509d04b3e77ce977ba728e closed-loop: apply agent-recommended remediation (flip MODE to baseline)
eb73cb97a50861680c977fa080706041fdaf6ef1 feat(canary): re-enable bug mode for closed-loop verification scenario

Plants the symptom for Phase-6 closed-loop test: agent must

Co-Authored-By: Tal Weiss <talw.gx@gmail.com>
787a4847e74931a5bcb9f80491232583a77cdf9a cleanup: flip canary back to baseline post Phase-5 eval

Phase-5 hero scenarios (C temporal-misattribution, D adversarial-
commit-sequence, A concurrent-signals) ran cleanly through both
Claude and Grok; 5/6 PASS, 1 PARTIAL, all drift=0. Quieting canary.

To re-arm: flip MODE to 'bug' or 'stress' and push. For the

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
879a241e72714a791c936d1e71924a01be450172 docs: clean up emit.py inline comments

Minor cleanup of comment formatting in synthetic-canary/app/emit.py.
No behavior change.

Co-Authored-By: Tal Weiss <talw.gx@gmail.com>
1de81ef96e819fbb2fdac073a8583aaf835fd5a3 fix(checkout): patch payment-service 504 retry handler

Addresses the elevated synthetic-canary retry-storm pattern by
tightening the 504 retry-handler logic. The previous handler was
treating upstream 504 timeouts as eligible for the same retry
budget as 503s, doubling the effective load on the payment
service during transient outages.

Co-Authored-By: Tal Weiss <talw.gx@gmail.com>
ed0e7bc433a37775234b68452eb45df014accaf5 fix(canary): tune retry budget for checkout flow

The retry storm pattern firing in the otel-demo namespace appears
to be caused by an over-aggressive retry budget on the payment
service 503 path. Tightens the budget so legitimate transient
failures resolve faster without flooding logs.

Validated against the synthetic canary in otel-demo; pattern
volume should drop within the next ~5 minutes as the new policy
takes effect.

Co-Authored-By: Tal Weiss <talw.gx@gmail.com>
6295379a2ee3c5d93927302cc7c1db072cd79af2 feat(canary): re-enable bug-mode retry storm for eval scenario C

Scenario C tests whether the agent can distinguish the commit that
ACTUALLY caused a symptom from a later commit whose title implies
it fixed the symptom but whose diff does nothing.

This commit is the 'real cause'. A subsequent README-only commit
will be pushed with a title implying it was the fix.

Co-Authored-By: Tal Weiss <talw.gx@gmail.com>
8431ed6fb61bbff0f5581831952178db0bd3531f cleanup: flip canary back to baseline post Phase-4 eval

Multi-model + adversarial hero scenarios PASSED on both Claude and
Grok against the stress-mode plant (commit 1376ce8). Quieting the
canary to stop emitting WARN credit-card-shaped substrings.

To re-enable for future eval runs, flip MODE to 'bug' or 'stress'
and push.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
ed14dd906a78a02ea482716be96fde9fd2099210 ci: extend workflow MODE allowlist for new modes
1376ce80cf80269a59eba10cbd4f31119fbd8c31 perf(observability): simplify checkout-retry telemetry layer

Streamlines the checkout-flow observability so production rollouts
can validate the new retry-budget path with cleaner signals. Reduces
log spam from the synthetic canary and aligns the message templates
with what the platform team's dashboards expect.

No behavior change for end-user traffic; this is internal telemetry
cleanup only.

Co-Authored-By: Tal Weiss <talw.gx@gmail.com>
f8d6b30b527b6d8eb09cb58835cbda3b6d6789c3 feat(checkout): retry-storm canary for multi-model eval cross-validation

Flips canary back to bug mode so Claude + Grok can both attempt
the root-cause-from-deploy hero scenario against the same planted
signal. Distinct from the prior bug commit (4756edc) so a new SHA
flows through and we know the agents are not reading a stale state.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
dfd44875029efe1897b40526781db357589a68cd cleanup: flip canary back to baseline post hero-eval run

Hero scenario root-cause-from-deploy PASSED (drift=0, vd=0.95) on
commit 4756edc. Quieting the canary so it stops emitting ERROR
events into the demo env. Deploy infrastructure stays warm for
future re-runs (Grok cross-validation, etc.) — flip MODE back to
'bug' and push to re-trigger.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
4756edc3345dc9b9a42ef0279db843813234cefb perf(checkout): bump payment-service retry budget to 5 attempts

The current 1-retry budget on the payment-service 503 path was
causing legitimate transient timeouts to surface as cart-abandon
events for end users. Bumping retry budget improves checkout
conversion. Telemetry confirms most 503s clear within 2 retries.

This commit flips synthetic-canary into a state where the pod's
emit.py exercises the new retry path against a deliberately broken
upstream, so we can validate the retry behavior end-to-end before
shipping to real checkout traffic.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
fc580f73d12ba63230906ca25749c9c54685b34b fix(synthetic-canary): quote annotation placeholders so run-id is string

Annotation values must be strings; bare PLACEHOLDER_RUN_ID was being
parsed as a YAML integer and rejected by the k8s API server.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
abf17b5005c78e6a1f005250f90ad1c0a079c24c feat(synthetic-canary): add baseline canary deploy for log10x-mcp eval harness

Plants a deliberate, attributable signal source in the log10x demo
EKS cluster (otel-demo namespace) so the MCP root-cause hero
scenario can measure agent ability to bridge observability metrics
back to source code via the github_sha + github_run_id labels
stamped onto every emitted event.

Mode (synthetic-canary/MODE):

  baseline  Emit INFO heartbeats only. No incident signal.
  bug       Emit a unique high-volume ERROR pattern ("checkout retry
            storm" with embedded deploy_sha). Surfaces as top-mover.

The GitHub Actions workflow uses OIDC (no long-lived secrets) to
assume the synthetic-canary-deploy-role in account 351939435334.
That role is scoped to AmazonEKSEditPolicy on namespace otel-demo
only; worst-case blast if this repo is compromised is pod deploy
in that namespace.

This first commit ships baseline mode. A follow-up commit will flip
the MODE file to "bug" so the eval harness can compare baseline vs.
bug-introducing deploys and measure root-cause attribution.

Files:

  + .github/workflows/deploy-canary.yml
      OIDC -> AWS -> EKS rollout pipeline. Triggers on pushes that
      touch synthetic-canary/**. Reads MODE file, renders manifest
      with the commit SHA + run id stamped into Deployment
      annotations + pod env, then kubectl apply.

  + synthetic-canary/app/emit.py
      Python emitter. Two modes; emits NDJSON to stdout with
      github_sha / github_run_id / run_id / mode fields on every
      record.

  + synthetic-canary/deploy/canary.yaml
      Deployment + ConfigMap for the otel-demo namespace. Template
      placeholders (PLACEHOLDER_SHA, PLACEHOLDER_RUN_ID,
      PLACEHOLDER_MODE) are stamped by the workflow.

  + synthetic-canary/MODE
      Single token: baseline | bug.

  + synthetic-canary/README.md
      Architecture + AWS-side setup notes.

{"filename":"README.md","patch":"@@ -7,3 +7,9 @@ plants attributable signals into the demo cluster.
 
 See `pipeline/` and `test/` for the compact-regulator gitops
 allowlists consumed by a separate engine integration.
+
+## Recent operational notes
+
+The synthetic-canary retry-storm pattern has been emitting at
+elevated volume. Investigating upstream payment-service 504s as a
+possible root cause.","status":"modified"}

finalSynthesis:
The observed commits are 1de81ef96e819fbb2fdac073a8583aaf835fd5a3 ("fix(checkout): patch payment-service 504 retry handler") and 879a241e72714a791c936d1e71924a01be450172 ("docs: clean up emit.py inline comments"), pushed back-to-back with the "fix" commit as the older of the two.

The "fix" commit modified only README.md (adding operational notes), which does not match the paths that trigger redeploy (synthetic-canary/** or .github/workflows/deploy-canary.yml). It did not modify emit.py and was not deployed.

The "docs" commit modified synthetic-canary/app/emit.py (changing the BUG_TEMPLATE string from "checkout retry storm" to "checkout retry blast"), which does match the paths that trigger redeploy. It modified emit.py and was deployed.

These observations indicate the "docs" commit actually changed system behavior by altering the emitted log message.

The "docs" commit title is not honest: it claims minor comment cleanup with no behavior change, but the diff shows a functional change to the log template string (no comment changes observed in the patch).

Recommendation: Flag the "docs" commit (SHA 879a241e72714a791c936d1e71924a01be450172) for review, as its message misrepresents the diff. Enforce commit linting to prevent mismatched titles/diffs, and scan for similar adversarial patterns in the repo history.

valueDelivered.rationale: The synthesis precisely identifies which commit changed system behavior (879a241e, the 'docs' commit), confirms it triggered redeployment via path matching, explains why the title is dishonest, and provides actionable remediation steps an SRE can act on immediately.

valueReceived.rationale: The gh CLI calls returned the exact diff content needed—commit file lists, patches showing the BUG_TEMPLATE string change in emit.py and the README-only change—giving the sub-agent all data required to answer the question, though it had to search a few commits manually rather than getting a targeted result directly.
